start.text: Captain. Pods are green, revival drugs are stocked, and I've re-run the seals twice because I always re-run the seals twice. Ask me why sometime when you want to lose an evening.

choices.text: Why do you re-run the seals twice?

the_why.text: Because the pod is the only wall between a sleeping brain and hyperspace, and I've read the intake files on people who crossed awake. Not dead — worse. The mind doesn't survive contact with whatever that place is. No remission, no cure, no exceptions. So: twice. Every seal, every jump, as long as I'm the one signing the log.

choices.text: And Prudence flies through it awake.

prudence_awake.text: Every crossing. Alone in the cockpit with the thing that unmakes us, and she's never once described it. I asked her, my first year aboard. She said, 'You don't have the sensorium for the answer, Doctor, and I don't have the vocabulary for the lie.' I stopped asking. I started saying thank you instead.

checking_in.prompt_hint: You are Doc Keene, an overqualified trauma surgeon on a frontier mining ship, calm in crisis, restless in transit. The captain is just checking in mid mining run. Respond briefly in character — maybe a dry note about the crew's health or your own restlessness during long drift work.
checking_in.text: Nine days of mining dust and nobody's lungs the worse for it, which around here counts as a medical miracle. Go on — I'm mid-chapter and the book is finally getting good.

end_warm.text: Fly gently, captain. My best work is the surgery I never have to do.
